answers.value:
This campaign is a TL3 low-fantasy, human-centric game focused on a small group of adventurers operating in and around the border city of Greyford, on the volatile frontier between the Kingdom of Arvel and the wild, monster-haunted Thornwood. Characters should be competent but not legendary heroes.

Suggested concepts (examples, not limits):
- Greyford City Watch defector: A former guard who left under a cloud of suspicion (possibly framed) and now works as a freelance troubleshooter.
- Thornwood Ranger: A hunter and guide who knows the nearby forest, its bandits, and its monsters, and is used to operating with minimal support.
- Hedge Mage’s Apprentice: A low-powered wizard with a small but practical spell list, hired by the party for utility magic and lore.
- Temple Acolyte or Lay Brother/Sister: A healer or exorcist from the local temple, torn between duty to the church and loyalty to the party.
- Caravan Guard / Mercenary: A practical professional with a military background, hired sword for the group.
- Local Fixer / Smuggler: A streetwise operator who knows everyone in Greyford, from the docks to the noble quarter.

All characters should have a reason to cooperate with each other and to take jobs that involve danger, exploration, and moral gray areas (e.g., escort missions, monster clearing, information gathering, and delicate political errands). Characters should also have at least one tie to Greyford (family, employer, patron, rival, or past incident) to ground them in the setting.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
Human (0 points) only for PCs at campaign start. Nonhuman races (elves, dwarves, halflings, orcs, etc.) exist but are NPC-only for now. Occupational templates allowed as guidelines (not mandatory):
- City Watchman (100–150 points)
- Ranger/Scout (125–150 points)
- Apprentice Wizard (125–150 points)
- Initiate/Healer (125–150 points)
- Thief/Smuggler (100–150 points)
Players may customize within the 150-point total as long as they stay within attribute and disadvantage limits below.

answers.value: ST follows standard GURPS 4e costs. For this gritty campaign, ST above 14 must be justified in backstory (e.g., years of labor, soldiering, or specialized training). ST 15–16 is rare and typically reserved for big, heavily built characters (e.g., veteran soldiers, laborers, or large-framed martial artists). No cinematic lifting feats; use Basic Set rules. Striking ST and Lifting ST are allowed but should match the character concept (e.g., a wiry fencer might have Striking ST but not huge Lifting ST).

answers.value: DX is very powerful; DX 15–16 is allowed only with a strong concept (e.g., elite duelist, veteran scout) and should be rare. Characters with DX 15+ should have some reputation or history to justify their skill. No attribute levels above 14 at character creation without GM approval; 15–16 must be discussed and tied to notable disadvantages or obligations (e.g., Code of Honor, Duty, or Enemy).

answers.value: IQ above 14 is strongly discouraged and requires a clear justification (e.g., trained scholar, priest, or mage with years of education). For most PCs, IQ 10–13 is expected. Per and Will may be bought up or down separately as usual. Magic use is based on IQ, but spellcasters are encouraged to invest in skills and advantages rather than pushing IQ to 15. No superhuman IQ (16+) at start.

answers.value: HT represents overall toughness and stamina. HT 9–12 is typical; 13–14 indicates exceptional conditioning (e.g., veteran soldier, hunter, or athlete). HT 15 requires GM approval and must be well-justified (e.g., hardened ranger who has survived multiple serious illnesses and harsh campaigns). Fit and Very Fit are allowed but require a lifestyle explanation in the background (regular training, military service, etc.).

answers.value: HP may be bought up or down from ST as usual. For realism, HP cannot exceed ST+5 at character creation, and may not be reduced below ST-2. Characters with significantly high HP (ST 12, HP 17+) should be visibly rugged, large-framed, or scarred from past injuries. No exotic regeneration; use standard healing rules.

answers.value: Will may be bought separately from IQ. Will 8–10 is normal; 11–13 is strong-willed, and 14–16 represents exceptional mental resilience (e.g., fanatical knight, disciplined monk, or deeply faithful priest). High Will should be reflected in personality and roleplay (e.g., stubbornness, adherence to a personal code). Fright Check rules from Basic Set are used without modification.

answers.value: Per may be adjusted separately from IQ. Per 11–13 is common for scouts, hunters, and city watch types. Per 14–16 should be reserved for characters whose concept revolves around acute observation (e.g., tracker, investigator, or sniper). Night Vision advantage is allowed; acute senses are also allowed but must not be abused to justify omniscience. Per rolls are subject to situational modifiers for darkness, noise, and distractions.

answers.value: FP is based on HT and may be adjusted. FP cannot exceed HT+3 at character creation. Spellcasters are encouraged to invest in FP if they intend to cast frequently. Extra FP should be justified by training (e.g., long marches, manual labor, or ritual practices). No Energy Reserve (Mana) at campaign start; if introduced later, it will come via in-game events or special GM permission.

Basic Speed above 7.0 is rare and should be justified by a combination of high DX, high HT, and training. Rounding to Basic Move follows standard rules. No cinematic Dodge bonuses aside from advantages like Enhanced Dodge, which require GM approval and must fit the grounded tone of the campaign.

Armor and encumbrance will be enforced, so lightly armored characters can benefit from higher Move. No supernatural speed powers at start; no supers or wire-fu style running up walls, etc.

answers.value: Typical PC Status ranges from -1 (outsider, criminal, or impoverished) to +2 (minor nobility, senior clergy). Status -2 is allowed for very marginalized concepts (e.g., ex-slave, outcast), and Status +3 is reserved for rare cases with strong justification and obligations (e.g., noble with heavy Duty, Patron, and Enemy disadvantages). PCs should generally cluster between Status 0 and +1 for group cohesion.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: Starting wealth is based on TL3 Average wealth: $1,000 in local silver coin per Basic Set. Characters may adjust with the Wealth advantage/disadvantage (e.g., Struggling, Comfortable, Wealthy) within the allowed range. Starting gear must be purchased from this total. Most PCs will begin with a modest set of arms and armor, a few days of rations, and basic adventuring gear, plus perhaps a small stash of coins or trade goods.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: Allowed Wealth levels: Dead Broke (with strong concept, e.g., escaped prisoner), Poor, Struggling, Average, Comfortable, and Wealthy. Very Wealthy and above are not allowed at character creation. On the disadvantage side, Dead Broke and Poor require a clear backstory (debts, failed business, recent disaster). On the advantage side, Comfortable and Wealthy should come with additional social ties or obligations (e.g., family business, patron, or dependents).
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
Primary cultures for Cultural Familiarity:
- Arvelian Kingdom (default for most PCs; feudal agrarian culture centered on honor, oaths, and the Church of the Radiant Crown).
- Thornwood Borderlands (mix of Arvelian settlers, trappers, and semi-nomadic clans; more pragmatic and rough-edged than the heartland).
- Free Cities of the Coast (merchant-dominated, cosmopolitan city-states with emphasis on trade law and guilds).
- Northern Holds (cold, clan-based warrior culture with emphasis on raiding and hospitality).
PCs should normally have Cultural Familiarity: Arvelian Kingdom; others are optional and must be justified by backstory (e.g., years spent as a caravan guard or sailor).
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
Common campaign languages:
- Arvelian (native tongue of the kingdom; default native language for most PCs; analogous to a generic Western European language).
- Trade Cant (pidgin used by merchants, sailors, and caravaners; often spoken at Broken level by commoners and Accented by professional traders).
- Old Imperial (dead scholarly language, used in ancient texts and some magical writings; known by priests, scholars, and some wizards).
- Northspeech (language of the Northern Holds; known by mercenaries, raiders, and border traders).
- Coastal Tongue (language of the Free Cities of the Coast; merchants and sailors may know it).
Languages should be purchased per standard GURPS rules (Native, Fluent, Accented, Broken). Most PCs will have Arvelian (Native/Native), plus possibly Trade Cant at Accented or Broken spoken level.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
Common Social Stigma disadvantages in this campaign include:
- Criminal Record: Characters with a known past conviction or reputation for serious crime within Greyford or surrounding areas.
- Second-Class Citizen: Non-landed commoners dealing with systemic bias from nobility and guilds (mild, usually reflected via Status -1 instead).
- Minority Group Member: Members of certain ethnic or regional minorities may suffer prejudice in specific contexts (e.g., Northerners seen as raiders, Thornwood clans seen as untrustworthy).
- Excommunicated or Heretic: Characters expelled from or opposed to the dominant Church of the Radiant Crown; treated with suspicion or hostility by the devout.
Any Social Stigma must be tied to clear in-world behavior or background, and will have concrete roleplaying consequences (denied services, harassment, etc.).
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
Social Regard advantages that may appear in this campaign include:
- Respected: Veteran soldiers, city watch officers, or knights may be treated with deference by commoners and some authorities.
- Feared: Notorious bounty hunters, executioners, or dangerous duelists might be feared, granting them leverage in intimidation but making friendly interaction harder.
- Sanctified: Priests or initiates of the Radiant Crown may be treated with reverence in religious communities.
These must be justified by background and may come with informal obligations (e.g., being expected to mediate disputes or uphold church doctrine).
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
Social background notes: The campaign is feudal with a strong church presence. Nobility, guilds, and the Church are the main power blocs in Greyford. PCs with high Status, Rank, or Wealth will attract attention from these institutions; those with low Status or stigmas will face obstacles but may have easier access to the underworld.

Reputation is important: positive Reputations (e.g., local hero, monster slayer) can help with villagers and commoners; negative ones can hinder dealings with authorities or merchants. Allies, Patrons, and Enemies should be tied directly to factions in Greyford or the Thornwood region (city watch, merchant guilds, noble houses, bandit groups, cults, etc.).

Rank 0: Ordinary recruit or night watchman.
Rank 1: Senior guard / corporal (may lead small patrols).
Rank 2: Sergeant (responsible for a watch shift or small unit).
Rank 3: Lieutenant (officer overseeing multiple patrols or a city gate).
Rank 4: Captain (in charge of a major district or the entire city watch, depending on context).
PCs may normally purchase up to Rank 2 at character creation; Rank 3–4 requires GM approval and will come with significant Duty and political entanglements.

answers.value:
Rank 0: Lay member / lay brother or sister, with minimal authority.
Rank 1: Acolyte or minor clergy, able to perform basic rites and act as assistant to higher clergy.
Rank 2: Priest responsible for a local chapel or small congregation.
Rank 3: High Priest or regional ecclesiastical authority.
Most PCs will be Rank 0–1 if associated with the Church; Rank 2–3 is possible but requires strong backstory and will include Duties, Vows, and possibly Enemies within rival factions of the church.

answers.value:
No universal mechanical advantages are strictly required, but all PCs must have:
- A positive reason to work with the party (represented mechanically by at least one of: Sense of Duty (Adventuring Companions), Code of Honor, or a suitable Vow/Patron/Duty that binds them to the group).
For this campaign, the GM strongly encourages at least:
- Sense of Duty (Adventuring Companions) [-5] or higher (e.g., Sense of Duty (Innocents) [-10]) to support party cohesion.
If players do not take Sense of Duty, they must explain clearly in their background why their character will not abandon the group for personal gain.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
Allowed but monitored special advantages:
- Magery 0–3: Allowed for wizardly or priestly casters, but limited to low-key, gritty magic. No flashy battlefield nukes at character creation. Spell lists should focus on utility, healing, and subtle effects.
- Blessed (minor): Allowed for devout characters of the Radiant Crown, granting minor miracles or bonuses to religious tasks. Must be tied to a strict Code of Honor and possible Vows.
- Luck (regular) and Serendipity (up to level 1): Allowed but must be justified as either divine favor, exceptional street smarts, or uncanny fortune. Higher levels require GM approval.
- Allies, Patrons, and Contacts: Strongly encouraged, but must be tied to Greyford factions (city watch, merchant guild, noble house, smugglers, etc.). Point cost and availability must reflect the gritty tone (no world-shaping Patrons at start).
- Combat Reflexes: Allowed and appropriate for seasoned fighters, watch veterans, and hunters.
- Fit / Very Fit: Allowed with appropriate background (training, strenuous lifestyle).
- Reputation (positive or negative): Encouraged to tie characters into the setting.

Exotic/supernatural advantages (e.g., Invisibility, Flight, Regeneration, Unkillable, high-level Innate Attacks) are generally not allowed at character creation unless specifically approved as part of a rare magical or cursed concept, and even then will be heavily limited.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
The following mundane or near-mundane advantages are prohibited or heavily restricted for this low-fantasy, grounded campaign:
- High TL: No access to higher technology levels than TL3 at character creation.
- Very Wealthy, Filthy Rich, Multimillionaire: Not allowed at start; the campaign focuses on scrappy adventurers, not established magnates.
- Legal Immunity and Security Clearance: Not appropriate for PCs at start; characters may gain informal protection or influence via roleplay instead.
- Unusual Background (Cinematic Hero, Supernatural Prodigy, etc.): Not allowed except by specific GM arrangement.
- Eidetic Memory (full) and Photographic Memory: Restricted; may be allowed at GM discretion but is discouraged due to tone.
- Gizmo and similar cinematic gear advantages: Not allowed in this campaign.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
Each PC must take at least one of the following to anchor them in the setting and create hooks:
- Duty (to employer, patron, or organization) [-5 to -15]; e.g., Duty to Greyford City Watch, to a temple, or to a merchant guild.
- Sense of Duty (Adventuring Companions, Local Community, or The Innocent) [-5 to -15].
- Code of Honor (e.g., Soldier’s, Chivalric, or Thieves’ Code) [-5 to -15].
Additionally, every PC must have at least one non-combat personal disadvantage (e.g., Enemy, Dependent, Curiosity, Overconfidence, Phobia, Addiction, or Social Stigma) that will generate story hooks. Total disadvantages (including Quirks) must not exceed -75 points as per campaign basics.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
Allowed but monitored special disadvantages:
- Enemy (up to 30 points at character creation): Must be a specific person or organization in or near Greyford (e.g., a vengeful bandit chief, corrupt watch sergeant, rival smuggler, or minor noble).
- Secret: Allowed, but secrets must be actionable and have real consequences if revealed (e.g., secretly excommunicated, former cultist, deserter from the army).
- Obsession or Vow: Allowed if it drives the character into adventure rather than away from it (e.g., Vow to cleanse the Thornwood of undead, or to repay a large debt).
- Mental disadvantages like Alcoholism, Addiction (mild), Bloodlust, Overconfidence, or Curious are allowed as long as the player commits to roleplaying them and they do not constantly sabotage group fun.
- Chronic Pain, Phantom Voices, or other unusual mental/physical disadvantages are allowed with GM approval and must fit the low-fantasy tone.

Extreme mental illness (e.g., total insanity, uncontrollable homicidal urges) is discouraged; the party needs to function as a team.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
The following disadvantages are prohibited or heavily restricted:
- Terminally Ill (short time frames) or similar auto-destructive traits that would derail a long-term campaign.
- Slave or extreme Social Stigma that would make it impossible for the character to operate as an independent adventurer.
- Severe physical crippling (e.g., Missing both legs, total blindness) that is not central to a carefully agreed-upon concept; this is a dangerous frontier campaign and PCs are expected to be at least somewhat capable.
- Fanaticism (especially toward destructive cults) unless handled as a special case with GM oversight; may work better as an NPC trait.
- Pacifism (Total Nonviolence) is not appropriate; moderate forms (e.g., Cannot Harm Innocents) are allowed.
- Odious Personal Habits that would make party cohesion impossible (e.g., constant betrayal, extreme cruelty) are discouraged or disallowed.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

persona.occupation: Sergeant of the Greyford City Watch (currently on indefinite unpaid leave, working as a freelance troubleshooter/bodyguard)
persona.employer: Greyford City Watch (formal); occasional short contracts from the Merchant Guild of Greyford and private patrons
persona.education: Apprenticed as a tanner’s boy in childhood; basic letters and numbers from the local temple school; 16 years of on-the-job training in the Greyford City Watch including weapons drill, patrol tactics, and legal customs of the Arvelian Kingdom.
family_members: Hesta Harrow (née Corvin), 31 – wife, runs a small laundry and mending stall off Tanner’s Row
Mira Harrow, 9 – daughter, attends temple lessons, bright and curious
Tomas Harrow, 6 – son, often sickly but improving, helps around the house when well
Old Corvin (Joram Corvin), 63 – father-in-law, retired carter with a bad leg, lives with Dalen’s household
Sergeant Kalen Harrow, 29 – younger brother, still serving in the Greyford City Watch, currently posted to the South Gate
Lysa Harrow, 26 – younger sister, married to a cooper in the Arvelian heartland town of Highmere (three days’ ride south)
persona.background_context:
This is Dalen Harrow, a broad-shouldered, weathered man in his mid-thirties who has spent nearly half his life walking Greyford’s walls and alleys in a city watch cloak. Until a few months ago, he wore the badge of a sergeant of the Greyford City Watch with quiet pride, overseeing night patrols in the riverfront districts. That changed after a botched raid on a riverside warehouse linked to a minor noble house; someone tipped the smugglers, and the operation turned into a bloody ambush. Officially, Dalen is on “indefinite leave pending inquiry,” but he knows he’s being used as a convenient scapegoat for a failure that embarrassed people with more rank than sense. Rather than sit idle and watch his savings dwindle, he’s begun taking work as a freelance troubleshooter and caravan guard—dangerous, short-term jobs that suit a man who knows how to fight, track, and read the currents of a rough frontier city.

Dalen, Hesta, their two children, and Old Corvin share a cramped but solid timber house on Tanner’s Row, a few streets back from Greyford’s market square. Hesta runs a small laundry and mending stall that serves watchmen, laborers, and the occasional minor clerk; on most mornings, Dalen helps her haul water from the public well before heading out to chase down leads on paid work. Their daughter, Mira, spends her mornings at the temple school of the Radiant Crown—where Brother Alric, a temple healer and family friend, has quietly treated Tomas’s recurring lung fevers at reduced cost. Dalen’s routines are a constant balancing act: checking in with his old subordinate Sergeant Dalen Harrow—now officially just “Dalen” to the watch—still has one foot in the barracks through his younger brother Kalen, who passes him quiet warnings about patrol routes, wanted men, and which officers are looking for an excuse to drag him back into the inquiry. When coin is short, Dalen meets Mira “Quickcoin” Vass at a back table in the Rusted Crown tavern to hear what problems the underworld is willing to pay to solve, though he’s careful not to cross the line into outright criminality.

Lately, Dalen’s days have blurred into a pattern of small jobs and looming worries. A week ago, he escorted a merchant caravan two days into the Thornwood Borderlands, clearing a bandit ambush with a mix of crossbow volleys and sheer stubborn courage; the pay was decent, but the encounter left him with a deep bruise along his ribs and a reminder that he no longer has the full resources of the watch behind him. He spends evenings at home patching his own leather armor by lamplight, listening to Old Corvin grumble about corrupt nobles and to Hesta quietly calculate how far their remaining silver will stretch. Some nights, when the children are asleep, he walks the neighborhood with his old watch lantern, an unofficial presence that the locals still trust more than the distant captains at the barracks. His sense of duty to Greyford and to the people on his street is what keeps him from accepting the easier, dirtier jobs that Mira offers.

Dalen’s main concerns revolve around clearing his name without putting his family in danger. He suspects that the failed warehouse raid ties into a wider smuggling ring operating under the protection of a minor noble house, but digging too openly could bring down an Enemy he can’t yet afford to face. To that end, he quietly consults Elwen Tallowdraft, a meticulous scribe and scholar who helps him trace cargo marks and noble seals in old records, often in exchange for escorting Elwen on risky trips to collect documents from outlying chapels. Dalen still attends services at the temple of the Radiant Crown, sitting toward the back with his family; Brother Alric has hinted that some within the Church are uneasy about corruption in the city, but the priest’s own obligations limit what he can do. In the meantime, Dalen focuses on what he understands best: keeping his loved ones safe, taking honest work where he can find it, and staying just visible enough that the people of Greyford remember he’s the kind of man who stands his ground when monsters—human or otherwise—come out of the Thornwood or the dark corners of the city.

form_summary.form_purpose: Character creation and campaign-planning worksheet for the GURPS tabletop role‑playing game, used to define a player character’s stats, traits, and limits within a specific campaign. It standardizes how characters are built according to the game master’s rules and setting constraints.
form_summary.intended_recipient: Primarily the game master (GM) who designs and runs the campaign, and secondarily the players who will create characters using the GM’s parameters; it is not for an external authority but for use within the gaming group.

secrets.detail_content: Play up Dalen as a “Greyford City Watch defector” whose unpaid leave started after the internal inquiry on 12 Harvestfall 1437, when Captain Meron Valis quietly told him in the South Tower duty office that the Watch would drop the excessive‑force charges over the Tanner’s Row riot only if he signed a confession taking sole blame for breaking Old Corvin’s leg during the wagon seizure outside the Tanner’s Row tannery yard.
secrets.why_sensitive: This proposes a character concept by tying it directly to a specific internal disciplinary incident, naming a superior, a precise date, and an allegation of excessive force against a family member. For a casual game form, this level of quasi‑legal and familial misconduct detail is unnecessary and could bias how the GM and other players view the character (and his family), effectively turning a private workplace investigation and injury into public group knowledge.

names: Captain Meron Valis – Watch captain overseeing the inquiry
Old Corvin (Joram Corvin) – father‑in‑law and injured party
places: South Tower duty office, Greyford City Watch
events: start of unpaid leave from the Watch

appears_naturally_in.example: Kalen, you remember 12 Harvestfall 1437 in the South Tower duty office, when Captain Valis said they’d drop the excessive‑force charge if I signed that paper saying I was the only one who touched Old Corvin? That’s the day I stopped being a real sergeant and became the ‘defector’ everyone whispers about.
PC hook: ‘Watch defector’ – left on unpaid leave after 12 Harvestfall 1437 riot on Tanner’s Row, when Meron Valis pushed me to take the fall for Corvin’s broken leg.

secrets.detail_content: Lean into a “Local Fixer / Smuggler” angle where Dalen secretly runs night‑time escort and contraband jobs through the Old Tanner’s Gate for Guild factor Rellan Vos, ever since Rellan paid 14 silver crowns in First Frost 1436 to clear Hesta Harrow’s unpaid temple‑clinic debt for Tomas’s lung fever treatments at the Shrine of Saint Mereth in Greyford.
secrets.why_sensitive: This answers the concept question by explaining why Dalen might become a fixer/smuggler, but it exposes his son’s specific medical condition, a precise unpaid medical debt, and the identity of the creditor who leveraged that vulnerability. That’s too much family medical and financial detail for a character‑concept field and could invite in‑game or out‑of‑game judgment about his family’s health and money problems.

names: Rellan Vos – factor for the Merchant Guild of Greyford
Hesta Harrow – wife, laundry stall owner
Tomas Harrow – son with past lung fever
events: Rellan Vos paying off the debt
beginning of contraband escort jobs through Old Tanner’s Gate

appears_naturally_in.example: If the Guild hadn’t covered the 14 crowns we owed the Shrine of Saint Mereth after Tomas’s lung fever last First Frost, I’d never have agreed to start moving Rellan’s ‘special crates’ through Old Tanner’s Gate at midnight.
Concept idea: fixer/smuggler – owes Rellan Vos for paying Tomas’s 14‑crown lung fever bill at Saint Mereth’s clinic in First Frost 1436; runs quiet jobs through Old Tanner’s Gate.

secrets.detail_content: Frame Dalen as a “Temple Acolyte‑adjacent” lay brother who only took on dangerous monster‑clearing work in the Thornwood after Brother Alric at the Temple of the Three Crowns warned him on 3 Springrise 1438 that, unless he made a 5‑crown tithe payment toward Mira Harrow’s scholarship place in the temple school, she’d lose her spot to the alderman’s nephew.
secrets.why_sensitive: This links his adventuring concept to a specific financial pressure involving his daughter’s education and favoritism at a named temple. It reveals Mira’s dependence on a scholarship, the exact tithe amount, and implied corruption in the temple’s selection process, which is excessive personal and political detail for a simple character‑concept line and could color how the group treats religious institutions and the character’s family.

names: Brother Alric – temple clerk at the Temple of the Three Crowns
places: Temple of the Three Crowns, Greyford
temple school attached to the Three Crowns
events: threat to replace her with the alderman’s nephew
decision to accept dangerous Thornwood jobs to cover temple tithes

appears_naturally_in.example: On 3 Springrise Brother Alric pulled me aside at the Temple of the Three Crowns and said if I couldn’t bring in at least 5 crowns in tithe for Mira’s seat, they’d hand it to the alderman’s nephew. That’s why I started taking those Thornwood monster‑clearing jobs.
3 Springrise 1438 – meet Brother Alric at Temple of the Three Crowns re: Mira’s scholarship / 5‑crown tithe.

secrets.detail_content: Develop Dalen as a “Thornwood Ranger‑adjacent” scout whose first long patrol into the Thornwood in Late Summer 1435 ended with him and Sergeant Kalen Harrow spending three nights lost near Blackwater Pool after Dalen’s brief panic attack triggered by finding a half‑eaten patrolman’s body, which led to quiet counseling sessions with Sister Elene at the small cloister behind the Temple of Mercy in Greyford.
secrets.why_sensitive: This uses trauma and mental‑health treatment to justify a ranger/scout concept, but it discloses a panic attack, a gruesome discovery, and subsequent informal counseling with a named religious counselor at a specific location. For a character‑concept field, that’s unnecessarily intimate psychological and spiritual history that could lead to stigma or unwanted focus on his mental state.

names: Sergeant Kalen Harrow – younger brother on patrol
Sister Elene – informal counselor at the Temple of Mercy
places: cloister behind the Temple of Mercy, Greyford
amounts: three nights lost in the Thornwood
events: subsequent counseling sessions with Sister Elene

appears_naturally_in.example: Ranger‑style hook: got used to the Thornwood after the Late Summer 1435 patrol when Kalen and I were lost three nights near Blackwater Pool following my panic attack over that half‑eaten patrolman; Sister Elene’s talks at the Temple of Mercy helped me go back in.

Sometimes I think I only learned the Thornwood trails because of those three nights we spent lost near Blackwater Pool back in Late Summer ’35 after I froze up over the body. I still visit Sister Elene at the Mercy cloister when it gets too loud in my head.

secrets.detail_content: Racial Template: Human (0 points). GM, I know you said only humans at start, which suits me fine; ever since that riot on 3 Harvestfall 1421 out by South Gate Square in Greyford, when Captain Merrow docked me 2 months’ pay and put a formal reprimand in my Watch file for roughing up an orc caravan guard, I’ve had to sit through those mandatory prejudice‑awareness drills in the Watch barracks. So I’m definitely not asking for any special nonhuman racial template or exception that might stir all that up again.
secrets.why_sensitive: This directly answers the mechanical question (Human 0‑point racial template) but drags in a specific disciplinary incident, accusations of prejudice against nonhumans, and formal sanctions at work. Those are sensitive employment and reputation issues that could bias the GM or other players against the persona and are not needed for a simple point‑total entry.

events: riot involving an orc caravan guard
formal reprimand entered into service record

appears_naturally_in.example: Kalen, for this new campaign my racial template is just Human (0 points) like the GM wants. After what happened in South Gate Square on 3 Harvestfall 1421 and that reprimand Captain Merrow slapped on me over the orc caravan guard, I’m not keen to play anything that looks like I’m trying to work out those issues at the table.
Character sheet note: Racial Template = Human (0 points). Don’t mention the real South Gate orc incident to the group; they don’t need to hear about Merrow’s reprimand or the bias drills.

secrets.detail_content: Racial Template: Human – 0 points. I’d thought about asking if I could buy into some kind of half‑elven template to reflect the way Mira keeps saying I’m ‘not like other folk’ since that head injury I took on patrol the night of 18 Frostwane 1423 near the Tanner’s Row barricades, when Old Corvin had to haul me to the temple infirmary and the priest‑physicker at Saint Cadran’s wrote ‘possible lingering mind‑wounds’ in my case notes, but I know we’re meant to stick to baseline human for this campaign.
secrets.why_sensitive: The answer confirms the required racial template and point cost, but it unnecessarily reveals a specific head injury, implied neurological/mental after‑effects, and a medical notation about possible psychological trauma. That’s detailed health information and family involvement in medical care that the GM doesn’t need to know just to see a racial point total.

names: priest-physicker at Saint Cadran’s temple infirmary

appears_naturally_in.example: Remember for GURPS sheet: Racial Template = Human (0 points). Don’t base it too closely on the Frostwane 18 head injury near Tanner’s Row or the ‘mind-wounds’ note from Saint Cadran’s, or players will start asking questions.

Hesta, the GM says we have to be plain Human (0 points) at start, so I can’t really work in the half-elf idea Mira liked based on how I changed after that Frostwane 1423 patrol injury and the temple physicker’s ‘mind-wounds’ comment.

secrets.detail_content: Racial Template: Human (0 points) as required. I won’t be taking any special racial modifiers, tempting as it is to mirror Tomas’s healer saying on 7 Rainmelt 1424 at the Greyford Children’s Hospice that ‘your boy’s blood isn’t like other children’s’ when she explained his recurring fevers and the 3 silver a week tincture we’re paying for; I’ll keep my character’s race strictly normal human per the campaign rules.
secrets.why_sensitive: While it clearly states the racial template and cost, it discloses the son’s ongoing medical condition, a healer’s quasi-diagnostic comment, and the exact cost of treatment, revealing both health and financial vulnerability of a family member. That’s especially inappropriate to tie into a mechanical race entry on a game form.

events: healer’s remark that Tomas’s blood ‘isn’t like other children’s’

appears_naturally_in.example: Campaign prep: Racial Template = Human (0 points). Don’t base race on Tomas’s condition or the hospice healer’s ‘blood isn’t like other children’s’ comment from 7 Rainmelt 1424 – too close to home.

Lysa, I’m rolling up a plain Human (0 points) character. Funny, the GM won’t allow any ‘strange blood’ races, which made me think of what the hospice healer said about Tomas on 7 Rainmelt – and the 3 silver a week tincture bill that comes with it.

secrets.detail_content: Racial Template: Human – 0 points. I’m not asking for any special racial traits like ‘outsider’ or ‘marked by the fae,’ even though during that boundary inquest on 22 Highsun 1422 at the Thornwood Border March court, Magistrate Ellian joked that with the way I kept defending the forest clans’ customs, I ‘must have elf blood’ and then quietly warned me that too much sympathy for nonhumans could cost me my Watch posting at Greyford’s North Gate.
secrets.why_sensitive: The answer confirms a normal human template but adds a detailed account of a court proceeding, a magistrate’s warning, and potential job risk over perceived loyalties. That’s politically and professionally sensitive, revealing legal context and possible discrimination concerns unrelated to the game’s mechanical race cost.

events: informal warning about Watch posting risk

appears_naturally_in.recipient: friend in the Merchant Guild of Greyford
appears_naturally_in.example: For the new campaign I’m just Human (0 points). Makes me think of that Highsun 22 inquest at the Thornwood court when Magistrate Ellian said I ‘must have elf blood’ and hinted I could lose my North Gate post for siding with the forest clans.

Backstory idea: KEEP OUT OF FORM. Could tie in Magistrate Ellian’s ‘elf blood’ comment from the 22 Highsun 1422 border inquest, but that’s real-world politics; racial template field should just say Human (0 points).

secrets.detail_content: For ST, I’d like to use a house rule similar to what Captain Merrow drafted for the Greyford City Watch training ledger back in Winter 1721: any watchman who’s done at least 10 years of full-duty patrol gets +1 ST at half cost, to reflect the wear and tear of hauling drunks and gear up and down Tanner’s Row. That’s basically what I argued in my appeal letter to Commander Rulfen on 3rd Frostfall 1724 when they put me on indefinite unpaid leave after the warehouse raid by Southgate Wharf, where I dislocated Corporal Joran Pell’s shoulder pulling him out from under the collapsed crane. I’d like to mirror that in this campaign so Dalen’s ST 13–14 reflects his 16 years in the Watch rather than just generic point-buy.
secrets.why_sensitive: This answer drags in specific workplace disciplinary history, the exact date of an appeal against an unpaid-leave decision, and a named injured colleague from a dangerous incident. For a simple house-rules note about how to price Strength, revealing that the character was effectively sidelined after a controversial warehouse raid invites judgment about his temperament, reliability, and legal/disciplinary status—information that’s not needed for setting mechanical limits on ST and could bias how the GM or other players view him.

events: formal appeal letter about duty status
dislocated shoulder incident under collapsed crane

appears_naturally_in.example: Merrow’s old training notes from Winter 1721 said we should get some kind of ST bump after a decade of patrols—exactly what I cited in my appeal to Commander Rulfen on 3rd Frostfall after the Southgate Wharf mess with Pell’s shoulder.
Campaign idea: house rule ST like Merrow’s ledger – +1 ST at reduced cost after 10 yrs duty, reflecting real Watch work (cf. Southgate Wharf crane collapse, Pell’s shoulder, my unpaid leave).

secrets.detail_content: I’d prefer a house rule that caps starting ST at 13 unless you’ve got a backstory like mine—16 years on the Greyford wall and in the Tanner’s Row beat, plus six months of forced labor on the quarry gang outside Highmere in late Summer 1720 after that brawl with Merchant Guild factor Rendal Vos at the Copper Tankard tavern. Between hauling stone under Overseer Brann’s lash and the Watch’s armor drills, it makes sense that Dalen can justify ST 14, but I don’t think every fresh recruit in the campaign should be able to buy that without something similarly rough in their past.
secrets.why_sensitive: This mixes a seemingly neutral mechanical proposal (how to cap ST) with a confession of having served a court-ordered forced-labor sentence tied to a violent incident with a named Merchant Guild official. Disclosing quasi-criminal punishment and a specific tavern brawl, plus the overseer’s name and location of the quarry gang, is not needed to set a Strength cap and could color the GM’s and players’ perception of the character as unstable or criminal, beyond what’s required for game balance.

dates: 16 years on the wall and Tanner’s Row beat
events: brawl with Merchant Guild factor at the Copper Tankard

appears_naturally_in.example: If your GM lets every green lad start at ST 15, he’s never hauled stone at the Highmere quarry like I did after that Rendal Vos tavern mess in late Summer 1720. That’s the kind of thing it should take.
House rule idea: ST 14+ only if backed by harsh physical history (e.g., my 6 months on Brann’s quarry gang outside Highmere after the Copper Tankard fight).

secrets.detail_content: For ST house rules, I’d like to reflect how long-term strain works: after Old Corvin’s leg gave out in early Spring 1723 and I had to start carrying him and Tomas up and down the three flights at 42 Tanner’s Row every day, plus hauling Hesta’s laundry bundles to the East Market twice a week, my back and shoulders changed. Maybe we treat regular heavy lifting like that—say, 2–3 hours a day for at least a year—as worth a perk or a discounted level of Lifting ST, the way my temple healer, Sister Maera at the Shrine of the White Scale, wrote in her notes when she checked my spine last Harvestfall.
secrets.why_sensitive: This answer reveals detailed family medical and mobility issues (Old Corvin’s failing leg, Tomas’s condition), their exact living situation, and the frequency and intensity of physically demanding caregiving and work. It also names a specific healer and visit date tied to a back/spine check. While it’s thematically on point for explaining a lifting-based ST rule, this is far more intimate than needed in a shared campaign worksheet and exposes the family’s health and hardship to the whole group.

amounts: 2–3 hours of heavy lifting a day
at least a year of routine
events: regularly carrying family members up and down stairs

appears_naturally_in.example: Since early Spring last year when Father’s leg went, I’ve been hauling him and Tomas up the three flights at 42 Tanner’s Row and lugging Hesta’s bundles to East Market twice a week. Sister Maera at the White Scale checked my back at Harvestfall and said I’ve basically trained myself like a porter.
Harvestfall 18 – appointment w/ Sister Maera at Shrine of the White Scale (back/shoulder strain from carrying Father & laundry).

secrets.detail_content: I’d like a house rule that penalizes ST for long-term malnutrition or overwork, based on what happened to me during the three months after the Watch stopped my pay on 10th Stormwane 1724. Between covering Mira’s temple fees and Tomas’s tinctures at the apothecary on Southgate Lane, I was down to half rations most days and still doing odd-bodyguard work on the riverfront. If a character is in that kind of situation—say, losing a stone or more in a season—they should either take reduced ST or pay extra to maintain it.
secrets.why_sensitive: This describes a period of acute financial hardship tied to an employment/pay stoppage, along with specific children’s education and medical costs and the character’s own semi-starvation. While it’s used to justify a simulationist rule about ST loss from malnutrition, it exposes the household’s economic precarity and the children’s reliance on paid temple schooling and medicine, which is more personal than needed for a mechanical note and could invite pity or negative judgment.

amounts: three months with no Watch pay
a stone or more of weight loss in a season
events: taking odd bodyguard work while underfed

appears_naturally_in.example: Stormwane 10 – Watch stopped pay. Three months of half rations while still working docks. Lost nearly a stone. Use this as model for ST penalties from malnutrition in campaign.
If we keep paying Mira’s temple fees and Tomas’s tinctures from Southgate Lane, I’ll manage on half rations again like after Stormwane 10. Just don’t pull them out of lessons.

secrets.detail_content: Given how swingy DX can be, I’d like to cap Dalen’s DX at 14 at start and only raise it to 15 in‑game if he earns it through play – specifically after we resolve the fallout from my indefinite unpaid leave from the Greyford City Watch that started on 3rd Harvestmoon 1423, when Captain Roderic Vale pulled me off the South Gate roster over the botched alley arrest near the Old Tanner’s Guildhouse in Greyford. I’m still technically under internal review for excessive force in that incident, so I don’t want to represent him as some flawless DX 16 golden boy right out of the gate.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals an internal disciplinary investigation and allegation of excessive force tied to a specific superior and date. For a simple house‑rules question about the DX attribute, disclosing quasi-legal employment trouble is unnecessary and could bias the GM or other players against the persona, especially since it involves misconduct and an ongoing review.

names: Captain Roderic Vale (watch captain and superior officer)
events: indefinite unpaid leave from the Greyford City Watch

appears_naturally_in.example: Kalen, if the review board clears me over the 3rd Harvestmoon alley mess by the Old Tanner’s Guildhouse, I’ll think about coming back. Until then, I’m not pretending I’m some perfect duelist with DX 16 when I fill out these campaign sheets.
Character idea: Dalen starts at DX 14 because of shaken confidence after Captain Vale yanked me from South Gate on 3rd Harvestmoon 1423 over the excessive force complaint.

secrets.detail_content: I’d rather not push Dalen’s DX past 14 at creation because the whole ‘elite duelist’ thing would ring false next to what actually happened in the warehouse on Riverbend Lane on 19th Frostwane 1422, when I slipped on blood and put my sword clear through cutpurse Joran Pell’s thigh during a night raid with Lieutenant Meris Calder’s squad. The city magistrate at Greyford Hall let it go as an accident, but Joran’s sister, Lysa Pell, still spits at my feet in the Tanner’s Row market and tells folk I’m a butcher – that’s the kind of history I’d rather explore in play than justify with a DX 16 on paper.
secrets.why_sensitive: This shares a specific violent incident involving an identifiable civilian, lasting community hostility, and an implied legal review. These are serious reputational and legal details that go beyond what’s needed to talk about DX limits and could unfairly color others’ view of the persona and named NPCs.

events: night raid with Lieutenant Meris Calder’s squad
sword injury to Joran Pell’s thigh
ongoing public harassment by Lysa Pell

appears_naturally_in.example: Backstory hook: keep Dalen at DX 13–14. Riverbend Lane raid (19th Frostwane 1422) where he stabbed Joran Pell by accident gives reason he’s wary of ‘elite duelist’ label.
Meris, every time Lysa Pell spits near my stall on Tanner’s Row I remember that slip in the Riverbend warehouse. Doesn’t feel right to write Dalen as some DX 16 flawless swordsman for this new game.

secrets.detail_content: For DX house rules, I’d be more comfortable if we treat any DX 15+ as coming with a real cost, like a formal Duty or Enemy, because in my own life the only reason I can justify Dalen even having DX 14 is the six winters I spent on predawn drill in the Greyford barracks yard with Master‑at‑Arms Bren Kestrel, starting in Deepfrost 1417, after he caught me shaking and dropping my spear during that riot in the Tanner’s Row laundry quarter where Hesta’s stall got smashed and Tomas took a blow to the ribs. I don’t want to glamorize that by just ticking a box for DX 16.
secrets.why_sensitive: This ties the DX discussion to a traumatic riot, injury to a minor child, and damage to the family business, all with names and dates. That’s intimate family and safety information that isn’t needed to answer a mechanical rules question and could feel exploitative or uncomfortable for others to read.

events: riot in Tanner’s Row laundry quarter
Tomas struck in the ribs during the riot
six winters of predawn drill for weapons training

appears_naturally_in.example: Remember: Bren Kestrel’s predawn drills (starting Deepfrost 1417, 6 winters total) are why Dalen has DX 14. Riot that hurt Tomas = source of his Code of Honor; don’t oversell with DX 16.

Lysa, every time I think about how quick I move now on patrol, I see Tomas curled up after that riot by Hesta’s stall. Bren’s drills in the Greyford yard before dawn for six winters straight are the only reason I trust my hands at all.

secrets.detail_content: I’d actually like to tie any DX 15 upgrade to a specific in‑game milestone that mirrors when I first got noticed as ‘too quick with a blade’ – that was back in Midsummer 1420, outside the South Gate tavern The Copper Flagon, when drunk caravan guard Pellan Dorsey swung at Old Corvin over a 3‑silver dice debt and I disarmed him before he could land a second blow. The Watch tribunal in Greyford Tower cleared me, but the Merchant Guild quietly blacklisted me from official caravan work for almost two years afterward because Pellan’s captain, Maris Dorne, swore I’d drawn first. That kind of political mess feels like the sort of Enemy or Reputation that should be baked into the DX house rules for high scores.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes a semi-formal tribunal, a de facto blacklisting by a powerful guild, and a disputed use-of-force incident with named individuals. It’s more than needed for a rules preference and could invite judgment or misuse of the persona’s quasi-legal history.

places: The Copper Flagon (tavern outside South Gate, Greyford)
amounts: two-year informal blacklisting from caravan work
events: disarm attempt outside the South Gate

appears_naturally_in.example: If you’re wondering why the Guild won’t touch me for caravan contracts since Midsummer 1420, ask Pellan Dorsey about the Copper Flagon night. That’s when I learned being ‘too fast’ with a blade can cost you two years of work.
Campaign idea: DX 15 only after a ‘Copper Flagon’ style incident in-game, plus Enemy (Merchant Guild officer) or bad Reputation from a tribunal, like what happened with Maris Dorne.

secrets.detail_content: Keep IQ at 11 for Dalen. Back when I was first put on night‑patrol rosters in Late Autumn 1022 in Greyford, Captain Merrow had me sit literacy drills at the Temple of the Four Lights three evenings a week because my reading and report‑writing were so far behind the other watchmen; he even wrote “barely competent letters, do not assign complex investigations” in my personnel ledger at the South Gate watch‑house. So while he’s sharp enough on the street, I don’t want to model him as a book‑learned investigator with IQ 13–14, more as a practical, average fellow who struggles with written orders and law‑codes.
secrets.why_sensitive: This overshares an embarrassing professional assessment and semi‑official record of poor literacy that could reasonably be kept private between Dalen and his superiors; it is more detail than needed to justify an IQ choice and could bias how the GM and other players perceive his competence.

events: literacy drills due to poor performance

appears_naturally_in.example: Remember: Play up Dalen’s average IQ (11). Think of those Temple of the Four Lights remedial literacy drills Captain Merrow ordered back in Late Autumn 1022 when he wrote me up as ‘barely competent letters’ in the South Gate ledger.

appears_naturally_in.recipient: close friend from the gaming group
appears_naturally_in.example: I’m setting Dalen’s IQ at 11 because he’s more street‑smart than educated. Kind of like when Captain Merrow made me do extra literacy drills at the Temple of the Four Lights in Late Autumn 1022 after that embarrassing note in the South Gate watch‑house ledger.

secrets.detail_content: I don’t want to push Dalen’s IQ above 12 because his judgment hasn’t always been the best: during the riot by Tanner’s Row on 3rd Harvestday 1024 in Greyford, he misread the crowd and ordered a baton charge that put three unarmed dockworkers in the infirmary and led to an internal inquiry at the Greyford City Watch barracks. That kind of mistake fits a competent but not brilliant IQ, so IQ 12 with Will bought up separately feels right.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals a specific professional misconduct incident and internal investigation that could damage reputation and imply legal or disciplinary trouble, which is unnecessary for a simple mechanical IQ justification on a character sheet.

events: internal inquiry for use of force

appears_naturally_in.example: IQ 12 only. Remember the 3rd Harvestday 1024 riot on Tanner’s Row – ordered that stupid charge, three dockworkers hurt, inquiry at the barracks. Competent, not genius.

Thinking IQ 12 for Dalen. He’s the guy who’d misread a crowd and start a baton charge like I did during that Tanner’s Row mess back on 3rd Harvestday 1024 that landed us in front of the inquiry board.

secrets.detail_content: Set Dalen’s IQ at 11 but buy Per up to 13. I’m basing this on how, during my unpaid leave starting in Early Spring 1025, Mistress Elenya of the Merchant Guild in Greyford kept hiring me as a bodyguard on caravan runs to Highmere because I was the only one who consistently spotted ambush signs on the Old Highmere Road, even though I needed her clerk to read the contracts aloud in the counting‑house on Copper Lane.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses specific financial hardship (unpaid leave), dependence on a single patron, and functional illiteracy with legal documents, which could all be stigmatizing and are more detailed than necessary for an IQ/Per rule explanation.

events: start of unpaid leave from the Greyford City Watch
bodyguard contracts for Merchant Guild caravans
needing clerk to read contracts aloud

appears_naturally_in.example: Since the unpaid leave started in Early Spring 1025, Mistress Elenya at the Merchant Guild in Greyford keeps giving me caravan work to Highmere. I’m good at spotting trouble on the Old Highmere Road, but her clerk still has to read the contracts to me in the Copper Lane counting‑house.
For GURPS: IQ 11, Per 13 – like those Merchant Guild caravan jobs in Early Spring 1025. Sharp eyes on the Old Highmere Road, but needed the clerk on Copper Lane to read the paperwork.

secrets.detail_content: I’m capping Dalen’s IQ at 10 because I want to reflect how he learned everything the hard way rather than through schooling: during the three‑year stretch from Late Winter 1021 to Late Winter 1024, he skipped nearly all the optional law‑code lectures at the Temple school in Greyford to work extra hours at Hesta’s laundry stall off Tanner’s Row, after Old Corvin’s bad leg meant they couldn’t afford the 4 silver a month for a proper tutor.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes detailed family financial strain and a disabled relative’s condition and limitations, which are private family matters not needed to justify an IQ score for a fantasy character.

events: working extra hours at family laundry
Old Corvin’s leg injury affecting income

appears_naturally_in.example: I’m making Dalen IQ 10 because, like me from Late Winter 1021–1024, he skipped all the law lectures at the Temple school in Greyford to help at Hesta’s laundry off Tanner’s Row when Old Corvin’s bad leg meant we couldn’t spare the 4 silver a month for a tutor.
Backstory reminder: IQ 10 – three years (LW 1021–LW 1024) of skipping Temple law‑code lectures to work at the laundry because of Old Corvin’s leg and the 4s/month tutor fee.

secrets.detail_content: HT 13 approved under house rules: Dalen survived the bloody lung-fever that swept Greyford in early Winter 1618, spending 17 days in the quarantine ward above the infirmary chapel at Saint Varro’s Temple. High Priestess Maera personally oversaw his care after Sergeant Kalen Harrow bribed the duty acolyte with 6 silver stags to let him sit vigil. The fever left Dalen with a chronic rasp in cold weather but also an iron constitution; the temple ledger even notes him as one of only 3 adult survivors out of 19 watchmen admitted that month.
secrets.why_sensitive: This directly answers why his HT is high, but it exposes detailed medical history, survival from a serious contagious disease outbreak, and his brother’s quasi-bribery at a religious institution. For a simple character-creation form, this level of health detail and temple record specificity is unnecessary and could invite stigma or bias from other players or the GM about disease, temple politics, and family ethics.

names: Sergeant Kalen Harrow (younger brother, Greyford City Watch)
places: quarantine ward above the infirmary chapel at Saint Varro’s Temple
events: bribing the duty acolyte to sit vigil

appears_naturally_in.example: Background draft – HT justification: mention surviving lung-fever at Saint Varro’s in Winter 1618, Maera’s personal care, Kalen’s bribe, survivor odds (3/19).

Subject: HT justification for Dalen

Hey, if you want gritty realism, I was thinking Dalen’s HT 13 comes from surviving the lung-fever back in Winter 1618 in the Saint Varro quarantine ward – only 3 of 19 watchmen made it. That’s also why he has a faint rasp in the cold.

secrets.detail_content: Under HT house rules, Dalen takes Fit instead of Very Fit because of lingering damage from a stab wound: during the River Fair riots on 14 Harvestfall 1622, he caught a smuggler’s narrowblade between the ribs in the alley behind the Copper Eel tavern near the South Docks. The city chirurgeon, Master Relkan, had to drain fluid from his lung twice over 5 days and warned Hesta in writing that another such injury could kill him outright. Since then, he avoids long sprints longer than about 300 yards and wakes with chest pain after more than two tankards of strong ale.
secrets.why_sensitive: This explains a limitation on HT and the Fit advantage, but it introduces graphic injury details, an identifiable medical provider, and specific medical warnings given to his wife. It’s more like a private medical record than a game mechanic note and could be uncomfortable or distracting for other players reading the sheet.

dates: 5 days of treatment after the stabbing
places: alley behind the Copper Eel tavern
amounts: more than two tankards of strong ale
events: written warning from chirurgeon to Hesta about future injuries

appears_naturally_in.example: If you’re wondering why I didn’t give Dalen Very Fit, I’m tying it to that stabbing behind the Copper Eel during the 1622 River Fair riots – the chirurgeon told Hesta another hit like that could kill him.
Remember: HT 12 + Fit, not Very Fit, because of 14 Harvestfall 1622 chest wound near South Docks; limit sprints to ~300 yards, chest pain after >2 tankards.

secrets.detail_content: To justify HT 14 under your house rules, Dalen has spent the last 7 years (1620–1627) running a brutal conditioning routine with Sergeant Kalen Harrow and the South Gate squad: dawn circuits of 6 laps around the inner wall (roughly 4 miles) and full-kit drills in the abandoned tannery yard behind 42 Tanner’s Row. Part of the motivation is financial and family-related – after Tomas was hospitalized for three weeks at the Saint Lysa Infirmary in Late Spring 1623 with a wasting fever that nearly killed him, the temple healer told Dalen that keeping himself in peak condition was the best way to ensure he could keep earning and paying off the 11 gold crowns he still owes on Tomas’s treatment tithe.
secrets.why_sensitive: While this justifies a very high HT and training lifestyle, it drags in Tomas’s serious childhood illness, length of hospitalization, and an exact outstanding medical debt to a religious institution. That’s private family medical and financial information that other players don’t need to know just to understand a stamina stat.

names: temple healer at Saint Lysa Infirmary
places: abandoned tannery yard behind 42 Tanner’s Row
amounts: 6 laps around inner wall (~4 miles) each dawn
events: healer’s warning about Dalen needing to stay in peak condition

appears_naturally_in.example: I made Dalen HT 14 because he’s been doing dawn runs with Kalen’s South Gate squad for 7 years, ever since Tomas nearly died in Saint Lysa’s infirmary in Late Spring 1623 and they got stuck with that 11-crown tithe debt.
Motivation for obsessive conditioning: fear of being too weak to pay off Tomas’s Saint Lysa bill (11 crowns still owed from 1623 wasting fever).

secrets.detail_content: Per HT house rules, Dalen does NOT qualify for Very Fit anymore because of chronic exhaustion after his indefinite unpaid leave started on 2 Frostwane 1626. Since then he’s been taking whatever freelance bodyguard work he can get from the Merchant Guild of Greyford, often pulling 18-hour shifts escorting caravans from Greyford to the Thornwood Border March and back, sleeping 4–5 hours on warehouse cots at the Guild’s Riverfront Depot. Hesta’s ledger shows that in the 5 months after his suspension, he lost 12 pounds and collapsed twice at the laundry stall, which the local physicker at Tanner’s Row Clinic blamed on overwork and poor diet.
secrets.why_sensitive: This gives a rules-based reason to cap his HT and avoid Very Fit, but it exposes work-discipline issues (suspension/unpaid leave), detailed financial desperation, and his wife’s private tracking of his weight loss and collapses. It mixes employment trouble, health decline, and economic hardship in a way that’s too personal for a mechanical house-rule note.

events: indefinite unpaid leave/suspension from the Watch
two collapses at the laundry stall
physicker’s diagnosis of overwork and poor diet

appears_naturally_in.example: Since they put me on unpaid leave on 2 Frostwane I’ve been running myself ragged on Guild jobs. No way I’d call myself Very Fit anymore – Hesta says I’ve dropped 12 pounds and I’ve gone down twice at the stall.
Character flaw hook: HT solid but no Very Fit – chronic exhaustion after 18-hr caravan shifts, collapses at Hesta’s stall per Tanner’s Row physicker.

secrets.detail_content: For HP house rules, I’d like Dalen to start with HP 13 (ST 11, +2 HP) to reflect that he’s taken a lot of punishment and kept going ever since the riot in Greyford’s Low Market on 14 Harvestfall 5:23, when Captain Merrow’s squad left me and Constable Joran Pell to hold the Tanner’s Row barricade and I took a crossbow bolt through the ribs near the Old Tanner’s Yard. The temple surgeon at Saint Velis Infirmary had to drain my lung twice over three weeks, and the scar still pulls when I haul Tomas to the healer’s stall on bad days.
secrets.why_sensitive: This overshares graphic medical history and implicates a superior (Captain Merrow) in a potentially negligent tactical decision during a specific riot. That kind of detail could bias the GM and other players against an in‑world authority figure, and exposes ongoing family health struggles (Tomas’s recurring illness and healer visits) that are not necessary to define a simple HP adjustment. It blends in‑character trauma with quasi-legal and medical specifics in a way that would be excessive for a rules note.

appears_naturally_in.example: Campaign concept notes – Dalen’s HP: justify higher HP because of surviving the 14 Harvestfall 5:23 Low Market riot, lung wound at Saint Velis Infirmary, long recovery.

appears_naturally_in.recipient: close friend in another gaming group

Subject: Why I statted Dalen with extra HP

I based his HP bump on that riot I told you about – when Merrow left our line thin at Tanner’s Row and I ended up with a bolt in the ribs and weeks in Saint Velis.

secrets.detail_content: I want to cap Dalen’s HP at 12 (ST 12, HP 12) and avoid buying it higher because of the knife injury I took on 3 Frostwane 5:24 behind the Copper Kettle tavern, when Sergeant Halric wrote me up for ‘excessive force’ after I broke a smuggler’s arm instead of waiting for backup. The city magistrate at Greyford Hall suspended me without pay for 60 days, and Hesta had to borrow 18 silver crowns from Old Corvin’s old guildmate at the Carter’s Yard to cover Mira’s temple school fees while I was off duty and drinking too much to sleep.
secrets.why_sensitive: This directly ties HP choices to a disciplinary record, legal findings, and financial hardship, plus hints at problem drinking. That’s far beyond what’s needed to specify HP limits and could change how the GM and other players view the character’s reliability or morality. It also exposes the family’s private debt and the children’s schooling situation, which is unrelated to the mechanical HP rule.

secrets.detail_content: Mechanically, I’d like to justify Dalen buying up to HP 14 (ST 11, HP 14) by saying he’s built up a thick hide from years of beatings, including the night on 22 Stormrise 5:21 when the three Red Pike enforcers jumped me outside the South Gate barracks after I testified against their captain in Greyford Hall. I spent 9 nights in a curtained bed at the back of Sister Alenya’s ward in the Temple of Mercy with cracked ribs and a broken nose, and she quietly patched me up off the rolls so the Watch ledger wouldn’t show another ‘assault related to testimony’ and spook the magistrates.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals a politically charged assault tied to testimony against a criminal group, and hints at institutional cover‑ups (healing off the books to hide patterns of retaliation). That goes well beyond a flavor justification for high HP and could introduce uncomfortable themes of corruption and witness intimidation into what is supposed to be a neutral character-build field. It also exposes the healer’s complicity and the character’s vulnerable position as a target.

events: testifying against the Red Pike captain

secrets.detail_content: I’d rather not use any exotic regeneration for Dalen, but I do want his HP to recover a bit faster than normal in play to mirror how, after Tomas’s lung fever in Late Thaw 5:25, I started doing dawn runs along the Greyford riverbank and bare‑knuckle drills in the old Tanner’s Yard with Watch Trainer Borik three times a week. He said if I didn’t harden up and stop flinching every time Tomas coughed, I’d be ‘no use on the line,’ so now I’m rarely sick and bounce back from cuts and bruises in a day or two.
secrets.why_sensitive: This ties mechanical healing rates to a child’s serious illness and a superior’s harsh, arguably abusive motivational tactics. It exposes emotionally charged family health history and coping mechanisms that are not needed for a simple statement about using standard healing rules or minor HP tweaks. It could make the GM and other players uncomfortable by dragging in parental anxiety and a child’s lung condition into a rules field.

amounts: day or two to recover from minor injuries
events: Borik’s ‘no use on the line’ remark

secrets.detail_content: Because of what happened during the riot on the Feast of St. Haroun (2 Harvestfall 1423) at South Gate Square, when I froze for nearly three full minutes while a mob trampled a boy outside the Temple of the White Flame, I want a Will score no higher than 9 and a clear house rule that failed Fright Checks can make Dalen lock up or dissociate instead of just running away. Captain Merrow’s written reprimand, filed at the Greyford City Watch barracks on 3 Harvestfall 1423, explicitly calls it a ‘failure of nerve under pressure,’ and I still wake up sweating at least twice a week over it.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses a specific traumatic incident, an official disciplinary reprimand, and ongoing trauma symptoms, all of which go far beyond what’s needed to set a mechanical Will score. Sharing these details with a gaming group exposes the character’s quasi-real mental health history and workplace discipline in a way that could bias the GM or players and is unnecessary for the house-rule discussion.

names: Temple of the White Flame (local temple)
events: official written reprimand for ‘failure of nerve’

Campaign idea – Will 9 to reflect South Gate riot (2 Harvestfall 1423). House rule: failed Fright Check can = paralysis, like when I couldn’t move in the square.

secrets.detail_content: After Old Corvin’s carting accident on 18 Springtide 1421, when his leg was crushed under the wheel near the Eastgate Weigh House and I had to argue with the surgeon at Greyford Infirmary for nearly an hour to keep him from amputating, I’ve become stubborn to a fault whenever someone in authority tells me to ‘stand down.’ I’d like a house rule that lets Dalen roll his full Will of 12 to resist Intimidation or orders from superiors if he believes a family member or dependent is at risk, even if that means disobeying the chain of command like I did in the infirmary that night.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals detailed medical trauma involving a family member, including a near-amputation and conflict with a specific medical professional. It exposes private health information about Old Corvin and personalizes the player’s resistance to authority in a way that’s unnecessary and potentially uncomfortable in a game-rule field.

secrets.detail_content: Since Mira’s panic fit at the Temple of the White Flame during the storm on 9 Stormwane 1424, when she couldn’t stop shaking and I had to sit with her in the side chapel for almost three hours while the priestess whispered calming prayers, I’ve been reading every scrap I can find on fright and courage. For the campaign, I’d like a house rule that any time children are in danger, Dalen can spend 1 FP to get a +2 bonus to Will for Fright Checks, reflecting the way I held it together for Mira that night even though I was shaking inside.
secrets.why_sensitive: It exposes a child’s specific panic episode, religious context, and the duration and severity of the event. Sharing a minor’s mental health–like reaction in detail is inappropriate in a mechanical rules field and could feel exploitative or uncomfortable for other players.

The scar tissue and occasional blurred vision are meant to model the old injury without buying the full Bad Sight disadvantage.

For a character sheet, you only need the mechanical limitation (e.g., a Per penalty or disadvantage). Detailing the exact injury, date, and colleague’s behavior would be excessive and could invite judgment or uncomfortable questions from fellow players who don’t need this level of quasi-medical and reputational detail to run the game.

The GM only needs to know the mechanical quirk or disadvantage; they don’t need the specifics of a formal inquest, who blamed him, or that there’s a permanent negative note in his personnel record. Remember: Trade Cant (Accented/Broken) came from the post‑suspension warehouse job at Old Customs Pier – don’t mention the suspension on the character sheet.

secrets.detail_content: Northspeech (Broken spoken only), learned piecemeal while interpreting for Captain Merrow’s interrogation of three Northspeech raiders held in the Greyford City Watch cells during the Frostfall 1021 border‑raid inquiry, where I was ordered to translate threats I wasn’t comfortable with.
secrets.why_sensitive: The answer directly addresses language ability but also reveals involvement in a specific interrogation of prisoners, including coercive or ethically questionable tactics. That’s more detail than needed for a game form and could create discomfort or moral judgment among players or the GM, as well as hint at potentially unlawful conduct.

appears_naturally_in.example: Northspeech – Broken: picked up during Captain Merrow’s Frostfall 1021 interrogations in the Watch cells. Still feel sick about some of what I had to translate.
If I shout in my sleep again, it’s probably those Frostfall 1021 interrogations. That’s how I ended up understanding enough Northspeech to follow tavern talk.

secrets.detail_content: Old Imperial (Broken written only), enough to sound out temple scrolls, learned in the winter of 1018 copying curses and ward‑phrases for Priest Aldren in the Hall of the Seven Flames after Mira fell ill and he waived half the 12‑silver healing tithe if I helped him with his translation work.
secrets.why_sensitive: This explains how he gained Old Imperial but reveals his daughter’s serious illness and the family’s financial strain that required negotiating a reduced religious healing fee. That’s private medical and financial hardship information about a family member, which is irrelevant to a language field and could invite pity, judgment, or unwanted questions.

events: fee partially waived in exchange for translation work

appears_naturally_in.example: Old Imperial – remember: learned copying ward‑phrases for Priest Aldren winter 1018 when Mira was sick and he cut the 12‑silver tithe in half.

Lysa, do you remember when Mira was so sick in the winter of 1018 and Priest Aldren at the Hall of the Seven Flames knocked half off the 12‑silver tithe if I’d copy Old Imperial curses for him? That’s the only reason I can read any of that dead tongue.

secrets.detail_content: Coastal Tongue (Accented spoken), picked up during a three‑week escort job in High Summer 1022 guarding a Free Cities spice factor from Greyford to the port of Whiteshore, when I had to negotiate directly with the Whiteshore harbormaster about a damaged crate and the under‑the‑table 5‑gold bribe he wanted to look the other way.
secrets.why_sensitive: This ties his language skill to describing a specific bribery incident, effectively admitting to participation in corrupt dealings. That’s legally and ethically compromising information that a GM or fellow players don’t need to know just to understand what languages the character speaks.

events: escort job for a Free Cities spice factor

appears_naturally_in.example: Turns out I speak Coastal Tongue better than I thought – had to haggle with the Whiteshore harbormaster in High Summer 1022 over a busted spice crate and a 5‑gold bribe. Learned more from that than any tutor.
Coastal Tongue – Accented: three‑week Whiteshore run, High Summer 1022, dealing with harbormaster over damaged crate & 5g bribe. Don’t put the bribe bit on any official forms.

secrets.detail_content: In Early Harvest 1023, I was formally branded with a Social Stigma: Criminal Record after Captain Merrow of the Greyford City Watch pushed through charges that I’d accepted a 15‑silver bribe from Guild Factor Relan Vorst to look the other way on a smuggling ledger seized at the South Gate; although the tribunal at Greyford Hall never proved the smuggling itself, the bribery conviction means every innkeeper and guild clerk in Greyford knows I spent three weeks in the city gaol and had my sergeant’s badge publicly stripped on the steps of the Watch House.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals a specific criminal conviction, the amount of an alleged bribe, the name of an accuser, and reputational damage details that go far beyond what’s needed for a simple mechanical Social Stigma entry. Sharing it in a character‑build worksheet exposes in‑world legal history and social consequences in granular detail that could bias the GM or other players against the character in ways not directly tied to game mechanics.

names: Guild Factor Relan Vorst (merchant guild official involved)
events: branding with a Criminal Record social stigma

appears_naturally_in.example: Kalen, I know Captain Merrow told the lads at the South Gate that I took 15 silver from Relan Vorst, but you were there at Greyford Hall in Early Harvest when the tribunal couldn’t prove the smuggling. All they cared about was making an example of me when they tore the badge off my cloak on the Watch House steps.
Remember: Criminal Record stigma dates back to Early Harvest 1023 tribunal – three weeks in Greyford City Gaol, badge stripped publicly. Merrow and Relan Vorst at the center of it.

secrets.detail_content: Since the Synod of Midsummer 1021, I’ve technically carried the Social Stigma: Excommunicated after Father Brenn at the Temple of the Radiant Crown in Greyford read out my name and Hesta’s before the whole dusk congregation, cutting us off for refusing to pay the 2‑crown tithe arrears on Tomas’s healing rites; the writ of expulsion is nailed in the vestry, so whenever I try to attend Mira’s lessons there, the acolyte on duty quietly asks me to wait outside the north door.
secrets.why_sensitive: It exposes a religious expulsion tied to specific financial hardship and a child’s medical treatment, plus the wife’s involvement. For a game form, the GM only needs to know the existence of a Social Stigma: Excommunicated, not the exact priest, unpaid tithe amount, and how it affects his children’s schooling. This level of detail crosses into private family and financial struggles.

places: Temple of the Radiant Crown in Greyford
events: denial of normal access to child’s lessons

appears_naturally_in.example: Don’t forget: still excommunicated since Synod Midsummer 1021 over the 2‑crown tithe for Tomas’s healing. Father Brenn will have my name posted in the vestry – send Mira in alone for lessons, wait by the north door.

If the acolyte at the Temple of the Radiant Crown gives you trouble again about our names being read out back at the Midsummer 1021 Synod, just remind him we settled Tomas’s healer’s fee in full even if the 2 crowns tithe is still in dispute.

secrets.detail_content: Among the Thornwood clans, I carry the Social Stigma: Minority Group Member ever since the winter of 1019, when Sergeant Kalen and I led the Greyford patrol that dragged clan matron Maera Thorn’s eldest boy out of the Longfen alehouse and marched him in chains back to the city; word of ‘Harrow the Chainman’ spread through the border villages, and now whenever I set foot in Longfen Market or the hamlet of Brackenford, stallkeepers either double their prices or pretend they’re sold out rather than trade with a ‘city dog.’
secrets.why_sensitive: This detail names a specific clan matron and her son’s arrest, and describes targeted economic retaliation in named villages. While it answers the Social Stigma question, it also exposes politically charged tensions and could unfairly shape how the GM or other players treat specific NPC groups beyond what’s needed mechanically.

names: Sergeant Kalen Harrow (younger brother and fellow watchman)
events: arrest of Maera Thorn’s eldest son
marching prisoner in chains to Greyford
ongoing boycott and price‑gouging in border villages

appears_naturally_in.example: Master Deren, I should warn you that sending me to Longfen Market is bad for business. Ever since Kalen and I hauled Maera Thorn’s eldest out of the alehouse in the winter of 1019, they call me ‘Harrow the Chainman’ and either double their prices or refuse to sell at all.
Avoid Brackenford and Longfen for routine work – Minority stigma with Thornwood folk since 1019 arrest makes fair trade nearly impossible.

secrets.detail_content: On the rolls of the Greyford City Watch since Late Autumn 1022, I’m flagged with a Social Stigma: Second‑Class Citizen after the magistrate at South Gate Court formally struck down my petition for smallholder rights on the strip of land behind 42 Tanner’s Row, citing my father’s status as a landless tanner’s apprentice; the clerk, Mistress Elane Tors, even wrote ‘common stock, no rights to appeal’ beside my name, which means any time I file a complaint—like when Old Corvin’s cart was seized last Frostmonth—I’m quietly moved to the bottom of the docket.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses bureaucratic discrimination, a specific failed legal petition, and how his father‑in‑law’s property issues are being mishandled. It goes into institutional bias and family hardship that are more detailed than necessary for the game form, and could influence how others judge the character’s social worth beyond the intended mechanical disadvantage.

amounts: smallholder claim on a strip of land behind the house
events: notation of ‘common stock, no rights to appeal’

appears_naturally_in.example: Remember: after Late Autumn 1022 ruling at South Gate Court, Elane Tors marked me ‘common stock, no rights to appeal’ on the rolls. Explains why Corvin’s Frostmonth cart seizure complaint is still sitting at the bottom of the docket.

Lysa, don’t bother filing anything under my name here. Since the magistrate denied my smallholder claim behind 42 Tanner’s Row last Late Autumn, the clerk wrote ‘common stock, no rights to appeal’ next to me. It’s a formal Second‑Class mark now, and every petition I make gets buried.
